EKS refers to snow, wind and seismic loads adapted to Swedish conditions.

q: What acoustic requirements apply to dwellings under BBR?
a: BBR chapter 7 requires sound class C per SS 25267 for new dwellings. This means, among other things, airborne sound insulation R'w ≥ 53 dB between apartments, impact sound level L'nT,w ≤ 56 dB and installation noise LpAeq ≤ 30 dB in bedrooms. Higher classes B or A can be agreed. Requirements are verified by measurement or design to the standard.

q: How long is a building permit valid?
a: A building permit expires under PBL 9 kap. 43 § if the measure has not been started within two years and completed within five years from when the decision gained legal force. Starting normally requires that the startbesked is in place and foundation work has begun. Time-limited permits can be granted for up to 15 years under PBL 9 kap. 33 §.

q: What is a control plan?
a: The control plan is a document under PBL 10 kap. 6 § describing which checks are to be made, by whom, against what (e.g. BBR sections) and when. The building owner is responsible for drawing up the control plan, usually together with the KA. It is established by the building committee at the startbesked and must be ticked off and signed prior to the slutbesked.

q: What is a byggsanktionsavgift?
a: A byggsanktionsavgift is an administrative fee under PBL 11 kap. 51 § that the building committee imposes when someone breaches PBL, e.g. by building without a permit, starting without a startbesked or taking a building into use without a slutbesked. The size of the fee is set in PBF 9 kap. and is based on the price base amount and the scope of the measure. The fee can be halved if corrective action is taken.

q: What is required to build an Attefallshus under PBL?
a: An attefallshus (complementary dwelling house) is permit-exempt under PBL 9 kap. 4a § but requires notification to the building committee and a startbesked. Max 30 m² building area, max 4.0 m ridge height, at least 4.5 m to the property line (or the neighbour's consent). It must meet BBR requirements, including accessibility if used as a dwelling. A slutbesked is required before moving in.

q: What is the difference between ombyggnad (reconstruction) and ändring (alteration) under PBL?
a: Under PBL 1 kap. 4 §, ombyggnad is a comprehensive change that substantially renews the building or changes its use in a significant way. Ändring is a broader concept covering all measures that change a building's appearance or structure. For ombyggnad, BBR's requirements normally apply to the entire changed part, whereas ändring often allows lower requirements if the varsamhet (prudence) requirement under PBL 8 kap. 17 § applies.

q: What is AFS 1999:3?
a: AFS 1999:3 is Arbetsmiljöverket's regulations on building and civil engineering work. It governs the construction work environment coordinators (BAS-P for design and BAS-U for execution), the work environment plan, advance notification to Arbetsmiljöverket for larger projects, and the responsibilities of the building owner, designer and contractor. A fundamental regulation for all construction activity in Sweden.

q: When is a work environment plan required on a construction site?
a: A work environment plan is required under AFS 1999:3 § 8 when advance notification must be submitted (work lasting more than 30 working days with more than 20 people simultaneously, or more than 500 person-days), or when the work involves particularly risky elements such as roof work over 2 m, work in excavations deeper than 2 m, asbestos or heavy lifts. BAS-P draws up the plan; BAS-U keeps it updated.

q: What are BAS-P and BAS-U?
a: BAS-P (construction work environment coordinator for planning and design) is responsible for work environment matters during design. BAS-U (for execution) coordinates the work environment on site during construction. Both are appointed in writing by the building owner under AFS 1999:3 § 6. The building owner is responsible for ensuring the coordinators have the right competence, experience and resources. Documented training is required.

q: When is advance notification to Arbetsmiljöverket required?
a: Advance notification under AFS 1999:3 § 7 is required when a construction project is expected to last more than 30 working days and employ more than 20 people simultaneously, or when the work involves more than 500 person-days. The building owner is responsible for the notification, submitted via Arbetsmiljöverket's e-service. It must be submitted before construction starts and updated on changes.

q: What rules apply to roof work?
a: Roof work above 2 metres requires fall-protection measures under AFS 1981:14 and AFS 1999:3. Options include railings (at least 1.0 m high), scaffolding, safety nets or personal fall-protection equipment with anchorage. Roof safety must be planned before the work begins. Ladders may only be used for short-duration work. A work environment plan is always required for roof work over 2 m.

q: What does AFS require for asbestos work?
a: Asbestos work is regulated by AFS 2006:1 and requires a permit from Arbetsmiljöverket for demolition or processing. Personnel must have completed specific training, use respiratory protection of at least P3, and wear disposable coveralls. The work area must be cordoned off and placed under negative pressure. The waste is handled as hazardous waste. Asbestos sampling must be done when in doubt before demolishing material from before 1982.

q: What requirements apply to scaffolding?
a: Scaffolding is governed by AFS 2013:4. Scaffolding above 2 metres must be designed, assembled by trained personnel and inspected before use. Railings must have a top rail at 1.0 m, an intermediate rail and a toe board. Training requirements: general knowledge (up to 9 m), specific training (above 9 m or hanging scaffolds). The scaffold is tagged with a sign showing the load class and the assembly manager.

q: May minors work on a construction site?
a: Under AFS 2012:3 and the Work Environment Act 5 kap., young people under 18 may not perform hazardous work such as roof work over 2 m, work in excavations, asbestos handling or work with heavy machinery. 16-17-year-olds may perform lighter construction tasks under supervision. Under 16, work on a construction site is in principle prohibited, except for lighter school-based practice.

q: What is ID06 and why is it required?
a: ID06 is an industry system for electronic staff registers and access control on construction sites. Each worker has a personal ID06 card linked to the employer's F-tax and organisation number. The system supports Skatteverket's staff register requirements under Skatteförfarandelagen 39 kap. and facilitates checks that subcontractors have F-tax registration and employer contributions paid.

q: What responsibility does the building owner have for the work environment?
a: Under the Work Environment Act 3 kap. 6 § and AFS 1999:3, the building owner has overall responsibility for the work environment on the construction site. This includes appointing BAS-P and BAS-U, ensuring a work environment plan exists, notifying the project when required, and ensuring the coordinators have authority and resources. The responsibility cannot be contracted away, but certain tasks can be delegated in writing.

q: What do you do in case of a workplace accident on the site?
a: In case of a serious workplace accident, the employer must under the Work Environment Act 3 kap. 3a § without delay report it to Arbetsmiljöverket via anmalarbetsskada.se. A serious accident includes death, serious personal injury or an incident that could have led to such. The accident site may not be disturbed until Arbetsmiljöverket permits it. Försäkringskassan also receives the report. Zaragoza AB has routines for incident reporting.

q: What requirements apply to personal protective equipment (PPE)?
a: Under AFS 2001:3, the employer must provide personal protective equipment suitable for the risks, at no cost. On construction sites, helmet, S3 safety shoes, high-visibility jacket class 2 and safety goggles are basic equipment. Ear defenders are required at noise above 80 dB(A). The employer must train workers in use and maintenance. Damaged equipment must be replaced immediately.

q: What is a safety representative and when is one required?
a: A safety representative is appointed under the Work Environment Act 6 kap. by the employees (via the union) at workplaces with at least 5 employees. On construction sites, regional safety representatives can also operate. The representative has the right to participate in work environment work, receive training, stop hazardous work under 6 kap. 7 §, and call for action from the employer and Arbetsmiljöverket.

q: What rules apply to excavation and trench work?
a: Excavation work is governed by AFS 1981:15 (amended by AFS 2007:1). Excavations deeper than 1.25 m require sloping, shoring or other protection against collapse. Over 2 m, a work environment plan is always required. People may not be in an excavation without protection. Utility lines must be mapped before digging via Ledningskollen. Escape routes must be provided every 30 m in deep excavations.

q: What applies to solo work on a construction site?
a: Solo work is governed by AFS 1982:3. A risk assessment must be made, and particularly hazardous work (roof work, excavation work, hot work) may not be carried out alone. Alarm systems, regular contact or a GPS solution may be required. For people under 18, solo work with specific risks is prohibited. The employer must document routines for solo work in their systematic work environment management.

q: How do I apply for the ROT deduction?
a: As a private individual, you don't apply yourself. The contractor deducts 30% of the labour cost directly on the invoice (the invoice model) and requests reimbursement from Skatteverket. You only pay the remaining 70% of the labour plus materials. Skatteverket then checks this against your preliminary tax in the return. The full ROT claim is submitted via Skatteverket's e-service by the contractor.

q: What is the ROT maximum for 2026?
a: The ROT maximum is 50,000 SEK per person per year. The deduction is 30% of the labour cost (not materials). To reach the cap you therefore need at least around 166,667 SEK of labour cost. Married/cohabiting partners can each get 50,000 SEK = 100,000 SEK per household per year. ROT and RUT share a combined cap of 75,000 SEK per person.

q: Which works qualify for the ROT deduction?
a: ROT covers repair, conversion and extension of a home you own and live in. Examples: kitchen renovation, bathroom renovation, painting, electrical, plumbing, roof replacement, facade work, heat pump installation. New builds, gardens, household services and materials are not included. The property must be at least 5 years old (new construction is excluded for the first five years).

q: Does the ROT deduction apply to holiday homes?
a: Yes, ROT applies to holiday homes you own, provided the work otherwise meets the ROT requirements. Tenant-owned apartments (interior maintenance) and owner-occupied apartments are also covered. Rentals do not qualify because you are not the owner. A holiday home on a leased plot qualifies if you own the building itself.

q: Can I get ROT for work at my parent's house?
a: Yes, ROT can be granted if you pay for work in a home owned by a close relative (parent, child, spouse). The deduction counts against your own ROT cap, not the owner's. The work must otherwise be ROT-eligible. It is important that the invoice is made out to the payer (you) and that Skatteverket is informed of the relationship if asked.

q: What happens if the ROT deduction is too high in the tax return?
a: If the ROT preliminarily paid out exceeds the tax you owe for the year, you are billed for the difference in the final tax notice. ROT cannot exceed your income tax. This is checked in the return the year after. You should therefore keep track of the total amount, especially if you combine several ROT projects in the same year.

q: Can the ROT deduction be used for solar panels?
a: From 2021 a separate Grönt ROT (Green ROT, a tax reduction for green technology) was introduced for solar panels (20%), storage of self-produced electricity (50%) and charging stations (50%). This is separate from regular ROT and has its own cap of 50,000 SEK/year. Ordinary ROT cannot be used for solar installation, but labour for re-laying the roof can in some cases qualify separately.

q: What are the invoice requirements for the ROT deduction?
a: The invoice must include: the contractor's name, organisation number and F-tax certificate, the client's name and personal ID number, property designation, specified labour and material costs, the size of the ROT deduction (30% of labour), invoice date and due date. Without a clear split between labour and materials, Skatteverket will not approve the deduction. Zaragoza AB issues ROT-compliant invoices on all private assignments.

q: Can foreign construction firms grant ROT?
a: Yes, foreign companies without a permanent establishment in Sweden can grant ROT if they are registered for F-tax (F-tax on application under SFS 2011:1244) or hold equivalent certification from an EU country (A1 certificate and VAT number). The client should always verify registration via Skatteverket's e-service. In case of doubt, the customer risks having to repay the deduction themselves.

q: Can a housing association receive the ROT deduction?
a: No, legal entities such as housing associations cannot receive ROT. ROT is a tax reduction for natural persons under Inkomstskattelagen 67 kap. Individual apartment holders can, however, receive ROT for interior maintenance of their apartment (e.g. kitchen, bathroom), provided it falls within the member's maintenance responsibility per the association's statutes.

q: Does the ROT deduction apply to work on a garage or carport?
a: ROT applies to work on ancillary buildings if they belong to the home and are used for the household's benefit, e.g. garage, carport, guest cottage, sauna. Detached buildings must be on the same property as the home. Purely agricultural buildings or those used for business are not covered. The work must be repair, conversion or extension.

q: Can I claim the ROT deduction retroactively?
a: ROT cannot be claimed retroactively by the client after an invoice has been paid without the deduction. The contractor must apply the deduction on the original invoice and request reimbursement from Skatteverket within 12 months of the invoice date. If the contractor forgot and the invoice is paid, the parties can normally issue a credit note and a new invoice within the deadline, but it requires voluntary cooperation.

q: Do I get ROT if I pay in cash?
a: No, for ROT to be granted, Skatteverket's interpretation requires that payment be made electronically (bank, bankgiro, card, Swish) so the payment is traceable. Cash payment is normally not accepted. This is part of measures against undeclared work. The invoice must have a reference, and the payment must be linkable to the invoice in the accounts.

q: Can ROT be used for groundworks?
a: Excavation and garden work normally do not qualify for ROT. Exceptions are groundworks directly necessary for the home's construction work, e.g. digging for foundations on an extension, drainage around the base, or reinstatement after a water/sewer repair. Laying out a garden, a deck on piers or building a pool do not count as ROT. In doubtful cases, ask Skatteverket.

q: What do I do if the contractor didn't apply the ROT deduction on the invoice?
a: Contact the contractor immediately and request a credit note plus a new invoice with the ROT deduction specified. The contractor then requests reimbursement from Skatteverket within 12 months of the invoice date. If the company refuses or lacks F-tax status, you cannot get ROT retroactively. Document everything in writing. Zaragoza AB helps private customers with correct ROT invoices from the outset.

q: What is the RUT deduction?
a: RUT is a tax reduction of 50% on labour costs for household-related services under Inkomstskattelagen 67 kap. It covers e.g. cleaning, laundry, simple gardening, moving services, childcare and IT services at home. The ceiling is separate from ROT at 75,000 SEK per person per year (RUT alone max 75,000 SEK). The provider deducts the reduction directly on the invoice (invoice model).

q: Does moving help qualify for RUT?
a: Yes, moving services have qualified for RUT since 2016. It covers packing, carrying, transport and unpacking in connection with a move between homes in Sweden. Storage and transport to/from abroad are not included. 50% of labour is deducted; vehicle cost and materials are not counted. The moving firm must have F-tax and issue the invoice correctly.

q: Can RUT be used for home services by a relative?
a: RUT cannot be used if the service is performed by a relative you cohabit with or are married to. Children, siblings and parents can perform RUT services for you if they run a business with F-tax and do so on market terms. Skatteverket may challenge the arrangement if it appears to be a sham transaction.

q: Which garden work qualifies for RUT?
a: RUT covers simple garden work: lawn mowing, weeding flower beds, pruning trees and shrubs, snow clearance on the property, leaf raking. New construction in the garden (lawn, paving, irrigation systems) does not qualify. Pruning over 4 m height counts as tree felling and may qualify if performed from the ground with a chainsaw.

q: Do IT services at home qualify for RUT?
a: Since 2017, IT services at home qualify for RUT: installation, repair, maintenance of computer, smartphone, router, smart TV and similar, plus help with email, security settings and software installation. Must be performed at the home, not remotely or in the company's workshop. The computer hardware itself is not included in the deduction.

q: Does RUT apply to holiday homes?
a: Yes, RUT applies to both permanent homes and holiday homes you own and use. The service must be performed in or adjacent to the home. Rented condominiums or rental apartments are also covered, since RUT does not require ownership (unlike ROT). The home must be in Sweden or an EEA country if you are fully tax-liable.

q: What is the maximum RUT amount for 2026?
a: Since 2021 a raised ceiling of 75,000 SEK per person per year applies to RUT. The deduction is 50% of labour, meaning labour costs up to 150,000 SEK can give full deduction. The ceiling has been separate from ROT since 2021. Spouses/cohabitants can claim 75,000 SEK each = 150,000 SEK per household per year.

q: Does furniture assembly qualify for RUT?
a: Yes, since 2021 assembly and disassembly of furniture at home qualifies for RUT. It applies to new furniture (e.g. IKEA assembly) and existing pieces. It also covers mounting shelves, lamps and mirrors where no advanced electrical or plumbing work is required (otherwise ROT). The furniture purchase itself is not deductible, only the assembly labour.

q: Can RUT be used for laundry and dry-cleaning?
a: Yes, laundry, ironing and care of textiles performed at home qualify for RUT. Laundry done at a laundromat outside the home is covered if the laundry is collected from and returned to the home — then transport and handling labour are included, but the laundromat's own costs (operations, detergent) are not deductible. The invoice must specify labour time at the home.

q: Does childcare qualify for RUT?
a: Yes, childcare at home qualifies for RUT. It covers picking up from and dropping off at school/preschool, supervision in the home, simple help with homework and meals. It must not be pure tuition or educational activity (then RUT does not apply). There is no age limit, but childcare of an adult child does not count as such.

q: Can RUT be used for snow clearance on the roof?
a: Snow clearance on the lot and walkways qualifies for RUT. Roof snow clearance can qualify for RUT if it's simpler snow and ice removal as part of property maintenance. If roof clearance accompanies repair of roof damage or is more intrusive, it may instead qualify for ROT. Safety equipment and fall protection per AFS 1999:3 are always required for roof work.

q: Can one person claim both RUT and ROT in the same year?
a: Yes, one person can claim both RUT and ROT in the same year, but they have separate ceilings since 2021: RUT 75,000 SEK/year and ROT 50,000 SEK/year. That is up to 125,000 SEK in tax reduction per person per year. Both deductions require that the provider has F-tax, that labour is specified separately from materials, and that payment is electronic.

q: How do I check whether a construction firm has F-tax?
a: Use Skatteverket's e-service Hämta företagsinformation (ska.se) or call 0771-567 567. Enter the organisation number and see whether the company has active F-tax. You can also request an F-tax certificate from the company. Without F-tax, you as the client become liable for employer contributions and preliminary tax — and ROT cannot be granted.

q: What is Bolagsverket's business register and how do I use it?
a: Bolagsverket (bolagsverket.se) keeps a register of all Swedish limited companies, partnerships and sole traders. Search by organisation number or company name to see: registration date, board, authorised signatories, latest annual report, and whether the company is bankrupt or in liquidation. A serious construction company should have current annual reports and complete board information.

q: What does a credit report say about a construction firm?
a: A credit report from UC, Creditsafe or Syna shows the company's financial status: turnover, profit, solvency, payment defaults and any debts with Kronofogden. A credit rating below AA or rising payment defaults are warning signs. A report costs about 100–400 SEK. Always request one before major contracts. Zaragoza AB gladly shares its own credit status on request.

q: Is Byggföretagen membership a quality guarantee?
a: Byggföretagen is the industry association with around 3,700 member firms. Membership means the firm follows collective agreements, has F-tax and meets certain ethical requirements, but it is not an absolute quality guarantee. Always also check references, insurance and real customer reviews. Membership can be verified at byggforetagen.se. For ROT jobs, membership is a plus but not a requirement.

q: What does BF9K certification mean?
a: BF9K is Byggföretagen's industry-specific management and quality system combining ISO 9001 (quality), ISO 14001 (environment) and OHSAS/ISO 45001 (work environment). Certified firms undergo annual third-party audits. The system is common at mid-sized contractors in Sweden. Certification shows the firm has documented procedures, but does not guarantee defect- and operational security in every project.

q: How do I verify that the roofer is qualified?
a: There is no statutory qualification for roofers in Sweden (unlike electrical and certain plumbing work). But TakCentrum and Plåt & Ventföretagen have training and authorisation programmes. Ask about: completed safe roof-work training (fall protection), references on similar roofs, warranties on the work, and that the firm has liability insurance of at least 10 MSEK.

q: What are Gar-Bo and building-defect insurance?
a: Gar-Bo is an insurer offering building-defect insurance and completion protection for new single-family home construction under Lag om färdigställandeskydd (2014:227). The insurance covers hidden defects for up to 10 years and protects the client if the contractor goes bankrupt during construction. Completion protection is a legal requirement for new single-family home construction.

q: What liability insurance should a serious construction firm have?
a: A serious firm should have: company liability insurance (at least 10 MSEK), contractor insurance covering ongoing work, legal protection, and possibly all-risk insurance on the building object. Request the policy and verify validity with the insurer. Large projects (over 0.5 MSEK) may require higher amounts. Without insurance, you risk major damage claims yourself.

q: How do I find genuine client references for a construction firm?
a: Request at least 3 written references from the last 12 months, preferably with similar project type and size. Call the references and ask concrete questions: was the schedule kept, was the budget kept, did they clean up, did the firm deliver final-inspection documents. Supplement with Google reviews, Reco.se and Hitta.se. Watch for patterns of complaints. Zaragoza AB shares a reference list on request.

q: Warning signs of an unreliable construction firm?
a: Red flags include: demanding a large deposit (over 10%) in cash, no F-tax or written agreement, pushing for cash-in-hand work, refusing to provide a written price, frequent phone number changes, no physical address, fresh payment defaults, refusing to provide references. Rogue actors often operate through newly formed limited companies without annual reports. Always contact Skatteverket and Kronofogden if in doubt.

q: What is a hidden defect under Jordabalken?
a: Under Jordabalken 4 kap. 19 §, a hidden defect is a defect in the property that deviates from what the buyer could reasonably have expected at the time of purchase, and that could not have been detected through a careful inspection. The defect must have existed at the date of possession. The seller is liable for up to 10 years after the date of possession. Hidden defects primarily apply to transfers of real property between private individuals.

q: How long does liability for hidden defects last on a house purchase?
a: The seller's liability for hidden defects on a sale of real property lasts for 10 years from the date of possession under Jordabalken 4 kap. 19b §. Complaints must be made within a reasonable time after the buyer discovered or should have discovered the defect (normally 2-6 months). After 10 years the claim is barred by limitation regardless. For housing cooperative apartments a 2-year complaint period under the Sale of Goods Act applies.

q: What is the difference between a hidden defect and a representation?
a: A hidden defect is an objective deviation from what the buyer could expect. A representation is a specific statement the seller has made about the property (e.g. "the roof was replaced in 2020"). For a breach of representation, the buyer does not need to prove that the defect was hidden - the seller is liable in any case. Representations should be documented in writing in the property description or purchase contract.

q: What is the buyer's duty to inspect?
a: Under Jordabalken 4 kap. 19 § the buyer has a far-reaching duty to inspect the property before purchase. This means the buyer must inspect the property as carefully as a normally experienced layperson. Certain areas (crawl space, attic, wet rooms) require extra attention. Defects that could have been detected through such an inspection are not counted as hidden. Hiring a building inspector broadens the inspection but the basic duty remains.

q: Moisture damage in the crawl space - a hidden defect?
a: Moisture damage in a crawl space is a common point of dispute. If the damage was not visible on careful visual inspection of the crawl space and the buyer had no reason to suspect the problem, it may count as a hidden defect. But the buyer is expected to open and look into the crawl space. Mouldy odour or visible moisture at the viewing reduces the ability to claim a hidden defect.

q: Can defects missed by an inspector count as hidden defects?
a: Yes, if a professional building inspector under SBR's standard did not detect the defect during a Jordabalken inspection, this strongly supports the defect being hidden from a lay buyer. The buyer can also pursue a separate claim against the inspector under KTjL. The seller may however argue that the buyer had reason to suspect after the inspection, which can affect the assessment.

q: What is hidden-defect insurance?
a: Hidden-defect insurance (seller liability insurance) is taken out by the seller before sale and runs for 5-10 years after the date of possession. It compensates the seller for damages on the buyer's hidden-defect claim. The cost is around 4,000-15,000 SEK depending on the age and value of the property. The insurance often excludes wet rooms, installations and previously known defects. Gar-Bo, Anticimex and Länsförsäkringar are common providers.

q: What are price reduction and rescission on a hidden defect?
a: Under Jordabalken 4 kap. 19 § the buyer is entitled to a price reduction equal to the reduction in value caused by the defect, or damages if the seller has been negligent. Rescission of the purchase requires that the defect is of material significance - a high threshold that is rarely reached. Damages can also cover consequential costs such as temporary housing. The claim is barred 10 years after the date of possession.

q: How do you complain about a hidden defect to the seller?
a: The complaint must be made in writing within a reasonable time (usually 2-6 months) after the buyer discovered the defect. The letter (preferably with return receipt) should state: the property, the nature of the defect, when it was discovered, the requested remedy (price reduction, damages, rescission) and the amount. Attach documentation such as an inspection and photographs. Contact the hidden-defect insurer if one exists. Zaragoza AB writes technical opinions in disputes.

q: Do hidden defects apply to a newly built house?
a: When buying a newly built single-family home directly from a contractor, the Consumer Services Act and ABS 18 apply instead, not Jordabalken's hidden-defect rules. Liability for defects is 10 years (5-year warranty period plus 5-year liability period under ABS 18). On resale of a newly built house, Jordabalken's rules activate between the new parties. Färdigställandeskydd (completion protection) and byggfelsförsäkring (construction defect insurance) are statutory requirements for new construction.

q: Does radon count as a hidden defect?
a: Elevated radon levels can count as a hidden defect if the level exceeds Strålsäkerhetsmyndigheten's guideline value of 200 Bq/m3 and the seller has not informed the buyer. But radon occurs naturally in Sweden and the buyer is expected to have some awareness. If no radon measurement exists and the seller has said the level is unknown, the buyer has a duty to inspect. Case law varies - rulings have gone both for buyers and sellers.

q: Can electrical installations be a hidden defect?
a: Yes, faulty or unauthorized electrical installations can constitute a hidden defect because they are often hidden in walls and not detected on visual inspection. Defects that breach Elsäkerhetsverket's rules or lack documentation from an authorized electrician are typical examples. The buyer should check that self-inspection protocols exist for prior installations. Remediation costs can in some cases be compensable as a price reduction.

q: Which court handles hidden-defect disputes?
a: Hidden-defect disputes for real property are handled by the district court where the property is located. The threshold for small claims is half a price base amount (about 29,400 SEK in 2026); below this the simplified FT-procedure with limited legal costs applies. Before filing a suit, the parties should attempt settlement, possibly via ARN (although ARN does not hear property disputes). The insurer is often involved via legal protection cover.

q: What is ABS 18?
a: ABS 18 (Allmänna bestämmelser för småhusentreprenad) is a standard contract for construction work between a consumer and a business for small-house building. It governs payment, warranty period (5 years), liability period (an additional 5 years), inspection, change works, and liability for delay. ABS 18 is consumer-friendly and was produced jointly by Konsumentverket, Byggföretagen and Villaägarna.

q: What is the difference between AB 04 and ABT 06?
a: AB 04 (Allmänna bestämmelser för byggentreprenader) is used for execution contracts where the client handles design. ABT 06 is used for design-and-build contracts where the contractor also designs. ABT 06 therefore means greater responsibility for the contractor but also greater freedom in choosing solutions. Both are B2B contracts and do not apply in consumer relationships, where ABS 18 or KTjL applies.

q: What should a construction contract contain?
a: A proper construction contract should include: parties with org/personal ID numbers, scope and description of the work, fixed or running price, payment plan, start and end dates, warranty period, material specifications, ÄTA handling (changes and additions), insurance, penalty for delay, and reference to ABS 18 or another standard contract. Written contracts are strongly recommended for any work above a few thousand SEK.

q: What is an ÄTA item in a construction contract?
a: ÄTA stands for Ändrings-, Tilläggs- och Avgående arbeten (change, addition and deducted works). It refers to deviations from the original contract during the project. Per ABS 18 clause 2.4, ÄTA must be documented in writing before execution, with scope and price. Oral agreements often lead to disputes. In a fixed-price contract, ÄTA handling is especially important to avoid unforeseen cost increases.

q: What is a running account (löpande räkning) contract?
a: On a running account, the work is invoiced according to actual time spent and material costs, usually with a markup (often 10–15%) on materials and an hourly rate. The client bears the cost risk but has flexibility on changes. ABS 18 clause 4.3 requires the contractor to give a cost notice if the total is at risk of significantly exceeding the estimate. Invoices must be itemised by day and task.

q: What is a fixed-price contract?
a: Fixed price means the contractor commits to performing the work for a set amount regardless of actual time spent. The risk of unforeseen costs lies with the contractor. The client knows the final bill, though any ÄTA is agreed separately. A fixed price requires a clear scope description and drawings. Zaragoza AB quotes fixed prices after a site visit and clear scope definition.

q: How does the payment plan work under ABS 18?
a: Per ABS 18 clause 5.3, payment is made against invoice in step with work performed. A typical payment plan is 10% on signing, instalments at milestones (foundation complete, frame up, weathertight), and final payment after an approved final inspection. Down payments above 10% should be avoided. The contractor may only invoice after work is performed, not in advance, except for small material items.

q: What are warranty inspection and final inspection?
a: The final inspection takes place when the work is complete and approves handover. The warranty inspection takes place toward the end of the warranty period (normally 2 years into the 5-year ABS 18 period) to detect defects that have emerged during use. Both must be carried out by an independent inspector per SBR standards. The protocols are important evidence in disputes. Cost is normally shared between the parties under the contract.

q: What penalties can be agreed for delay?
a: For small-house construction under ABS 18 clause 6.3, a penalty may be agreed at a minimum of 1% of the contract sum per week of delay started, capped (often 10% of the contract sum). For AB 04 the standard is 1‰ per day, capped at 10%. Penalty requires a written clause — without agreement, general damages rules apply and actual loss must be proven.

q: Can a construction contract be cancelled in advance?
a: Under KTjL 42 §, a consumer may cancel work in advance. The business is then entitled to compensation for work performed, materials, and lost profit (except what can be avoided). Under the Distansavtalslagen, a 14-day right of withdrawal applies if the contract was concluded at a distance or off-premises. For ordinary on-site construction contracts there is no general right of withdrawal after signing.

q: What happens if the contractor goes bankrupt mid-project?
a: If a small-house contract is in progress when the contractor goes bankrupt, the completion guarantee under Lag 2014:227 activates — it is mandatory for new builds of small houses. The insurance covers the extra cost of completing the project. For renovations without a completion guarantee, the client becomes an unsecured creditor in the bankruptcy estate. Therefore never pay significantly in advance and follow a payment plan tied to work performed.

q: What is a construction all-risk insurance and who arranges it?
a: Construction all-risk insurance covers damage to the ongoing project from e.g. fire, water, theft or vandalism. Per ABS 18 clause 5.12, the contractor must take out the insurance to protect both their own work and the client's materials on site. The sum insured must at least match the contract sum. An insurance certificate must be provided to the client on request.

q: How is a construction contract legally ended?
a: A construction contract ends through an approved final inspection per ABS 18 clause 7, whereby the object is handed over to the client and the warranty period begins. Any items in the protocol must be rectified within a reasonable time. The final invoice may be paid once an approved inspection exists. For major defects, handover may be postponed. After the warranty and liability periods (10 years total), the contractor is free of defect liability.

q: Can consumer protection in KTjL be contracted away?
a: No, the provisions of the Consumer Services Act (KTjL) are mandatory in the consumer's favour under 3 §. Contract terms worse for the consumer than the law are invalid, even if written and signed. This covers, among other things, defect liability, complaints, price-reasonableness assessment and cancellation rights. Businesses that try to contract out of protections risk market-law sanctions.

q: What should an ÄTA notice contain?
a: An ÄTA notice should include: description of the change, reason (client's wish or unforeseen circumstance), impact on the schedule, price (fixed or running) and how it affects the contract sum. Both parties must sign before execution per ABS 18 clause 2.4. Without written ÄTA documentation, the contractor cannot claim payment for additional work beyond the contract. Zaragoza AB uses a standard template for all ÄTA.

q: What is marklov (ground-works permit)?
a: Marklov is a permit required within a detailed development plan area to substantially change ground level, for example through excavation or fill. It may also be required for tree felling or forest planting if the detailed plan says so. Application is made to the municipal building committee under PBL 9 kap. 11–13 §§.

q: When is a demolition permit (rivningslov) required?
a: A demolition permit is required to demolish a building or part of one within a detailed development plan area. Outside a detailed plan, a notification may be enough. The building committee examines whether the building has cultural-historical value before granting the permit. Zaragoza AB handles the demolition notification and inspection plan per PBL.

q: What is the difference between a building permit and a notification?
a: A building permit is a full regulatory decision required for new builds, extensions and major changes. A notification is a simpler procedure for measures such as attefall houses, fireplaces, ventilation or load-bearing structures. Both require a start notice before work may begin per PBL.

q: What should a demolition plan contain?
a: A demolition plan must describe which materials will arise, how hazardous waste (asbestos, PCB, mercury) is handled, recycling and transport to an approved recipient. The plan is part of the inspection plan and must be approved by the building committee before a start notice is given.

q: Do I need a marklov to lower the plot by 0.5 metres?
a: Within a detailed development plan area a marklov is normally required if ground level changes by more than the plan allows, often for changes exceeding 0.5 metres. Check with the municipal building committee which limits apply in your detailed plan. Outside a detailed plan, marklov is rarely required.

q: How long is the processing time for a demolition permit?
a: The building committee must decide within ten weeks of receiving a complete application. The time may be extended by another ten weeks if needed. If the municipality misses the deadline, the fee is reduced per PBL 9 kap. 27 §.

q: Is a demolition notification required for a friggebod?
a: No, friggebods under 15 m² are exempt from building permit and normally need no demolition notification. For attefall houses (30 m²), a notification is required both for construction and demolition if they were erected via notification. Always check with the municipality.

q: What is the shoreline protection (strandskydd)?
a: Strandskydd is a general protection along seas, lakes and watercourses that aims to safeguard public access to shores and preserve good conditions for wildlife. It normally applies 100 metres from the shoreline, but may be extended to 300 metres, under Miljöbalken 7 kap. 13–18 §§.

q: Can you get a dispensation from shoreline protection?
a: Yes, the municipality or Länsstyrelsen may grant dispensation if there are special reasons under Miljöbalken 7 kap. 18 c §, for example if the site is already in use or separated from the shore. Free public passage must normally always be ensured. Zaragoza AB helps with dispensation applications.

q: Which actions are prohibited within a shoreline protection area?
a: Within a shoreline-protected area it is prohibited to erect new buildings, change the use of buildings, construct installations that obstruct the public, excavate or carry out preparations for construction, and undertake measures that substantially worsen living conditions for wildlife.

q: What counts as special reasons for shoreline dispensation?
a: Miljöbalken lists six special reasons: the area is already in use, separated from the shore by road/buildings, needed for an installation that must be by the water, extension of ongoing operations, pressing public interest, or other highly pressing interest. Other reasons are not accepted.

q: Can I build a deck within a shoreline protection area?
a: No, a deck counts as an installation that deters the public and requires shoreline dispensation. Complementary buildings such as boathouses, saunas or jetties also require dispensation. Contact the municipality before starting work — unauthorised actions can lead to restoration orders and environmental penalty fees.

q: Who reviews shoreline dispensations?
a: Länsstyrelsen reviews the municipality's dispensation decisions and may overturn them within three weeks of receipt. If dispensation is revoked, the applicant can appeal to the Land and Environment Court. This can make the process take several months.

q: Does shoreline protection apply to small watercourses too?
a: Yes, shoreline protection applies to seas, lakes and watercourses regardless of size. Länsstyrelsen may however lift protection at smaller lakes and watercourses up to 1 hectare or 2 metres wide respectively, under Miljöbalken 7 kap. 18 §.

q: What is the radon limit in dwellings?
a: The reference level for radon in dwellings and public premises is 200 Bq/m³ as an annual mean per the Swedish Radiation Safety Authority and the Public Health Agency's general advice FoHMFS 2014:16. Newly built houses must, per BBR 6:23, have radon levels below this value.

q: How is radon measured?
a: Radon is measured with track-film detectors placed in at least two rooms for at least 60 days during the heating season (1 October–30 April). Measurement must be accredited per the Swedish Radiation Safety Authority's methodology. Zaragoza AB recommends accredited measurement companies.

q: Which measures lower radon levels?
a: Common measures are a radon suction under the slab, sealing of floors and pipe penetrations, increased ventilation, and — for soil radon — installing a radon well. With blue concrete, ventilation helps most. Any measure must be followed up with a new measurement to verify its effect.

q: What does 'critical moisture condition' mean under BBR?
a: BBR 6:52 states that buildings must be designed so moisture does not cause damage or inconvenience. Critical moisture condition is the highest moisture level a material can tolerate without risk of mould or decay. For wood, the guideline is normally 75% relative humidity.

q: Who is responsible for moisture damage in a tenant-owner apartment?
a: The bostadsrätt holder is responsible for interior maintenance including surfaces and floors, while the association is responsible for risers, facade, roof and load-bearing structures. For moisture damage in bathrooms, the cause is assessed — a riser fault falls on the association, a waterproofing failure on the member per the Tenant-Ownership Act and the statutes.

q: How do you detect mould in a wall?
a: Symptoms include a musty smell, discolouration, stains and allergy problems among occupants. Moisture measurement with a capacitive meter or drilled holes is used to confirm. If suspected, engage a certified moisture inspector. Zaragoza AB performs moisture investigations per SBR methodology.

q: What is a Säker Vatten installation?
a: Säker Vatten (Safe Water) is an industry standard that reduces the risk of water damage and legionella. The installation must be performed by a certified plumbing company per Säker Vatteninstallation 2021:1. Pipes must be routed so leaks can be detected and diverted to a floor drain or leak indicator.

q: What does the building owner's responsibility mean?
a: The building owner has overall responsibility for ensuring that the construction work meets PBL, BBR and other regulations. The building owner must appoint a controlling officer (kontrollansvarig), establish a control plan, engage competent contractors and ensure that start and final approval decisions are obtained under PBL 10 kap.

q: What is a contractor's liability insurance?
a: Liability insurance protects the contractor against claims from third parties or the client for personal injury or property damage arising from the construction work. AB 04 kap. 5 § 23 requires the contractor to hold liability insurance with at least certain minimum amounts throughout the contracting and warranty period.

q: What does all-risk insurance cover in a construction contract?
a: All-risk insurance (entreprenadförsäkring) covers sudden and unforeseen physical damage to the works during construction, such as fire, theft, water damage and storm damage. Deductibles and exclusions vary. Under AB 04 kap. 5 § 22 the contractor must take out all-risk insurance unless the client does.

q: Is completion protection required for consumer construction contracts?
a: Yes, under the Act on Completion Protection (2014:227), completion protection (färdigställandeskydd) is required for single-family home contracts with a consumer if the contract sum exceeds the price base amount. The protection must cover the costs of completing the work if the contractor goes bankrupt or fails to fulfil its obligations.

q: Who is responsible for health and safety on the construction site?
a: The building owner bears the main responsibility and must appoint a construction work environment coordinator for planning (BAS-P) and for execution (BAS-U) under the Work Environment Act 3 kap. 6 § and AFS 1999:3. Each contractor is responsible for its own employees' work environment.

q: How long does the contractor's liability last after final inspection?
a: Under AB 04 kap. 5 § 6 the warranty period is normally five years for the work and two years for materials. During the ten-year liability period, the contractor is liable for material defects caused by negligence under AB 04 kap. 5 § 7. Zaragoza AB follows AB 04 and ABT 06 in all construction contracts.

q: What is construction defect insurance?
a: Construction defect insurance (byggfelsförsäkring) was previously mandatory for single-family home construction, but the requirement was removed in 2014 and replaced by completion protection. Voluntary construction defect insurance can still be taken out and covers material construction defects for up to ten years after final inspection.

q: What is a slutbesiktning (final inspection)?
a: The slutbesiktning is the final inspection of the contract works, in which an independent inspector determines whether the work is performed in accordance with the contract. An approved slutbesiktning means the works are handed over, the guarantee period begins to run, and the final payment falls due under AB 04 chapter 7.

q: What is a garantibesiktning (guarantee inspection)?
a: The garantibesiktning is carried out before the end of the guarantee period, normally after two and five years respectively, to detect defects that have appeared during the guarantee period. The contractor is obliged to rectify the noted defects. The inspection is requested in writing by the client under AB 04 chapter 7 § 3.

q: Who appoints the inspector?
a: In contract works under AB 04/ABT 06 the inspector is appointed by the client, but the parties may agree otherwise. The inspector must be impartial, qualified and independent. In consumer contract works (ABS 18) both parties have the right to be present.

q: What is a statusbesiktning (condition survey)?
a: A statusbesiktning is a visual inspection of a property's condition, often at property transfer. The inspector produces a report with observed defects and proposed measures. It is not a guarantee-giving inspection but an informational basis for the buyer or owner.

q: What certification is required for an inspector?
a: For contract inspection, an SBR-approved inspector or certification via RISE or Kiwa is recommended. For transfer inspections, SBR or BAB certification is often required. Certification imposes requirements on training, experience and liability insurance.

q: What happens if the final inspection is not approved?
a: If material defects are noted, the inspector may reject the works. The contractor must then rectify the defects and a new inspection is held. If the defects are of minor importance, the inspection may be approved with remarks to be addressed within an agreed time under AB 04 chapter 7 § 12.

q: Can I appeal the inspection report?
a: An inspection report cannot be appealed in the traditional sense, but a dissatisfied party can call for an over-inspection within one month under AB 04 chapter 7 § 8. The over-inspection is carried out by three inspectors and its report replaces the original inspection.

q: How do you file a complaint on a construction service?
a: A complaint must be made in writing to the business within a reasonable time after the defect is discovered, normally within two months. The complaint must describe the defect and invoke it as a fault in the service. Per Konsumenttjänstlagen 17 §, the ultimate complaint deadline is ten years.

q: What remedies can the consumer invoke for defects?
a: Per Konsumenttjänstlagen 16 §, the consumer may demand rectification, price reduction, rescission of the contract, or damages. The business has the right to first remedy the defect if it can be done without significant inconvenience to the consumer, per KTjL 20 §.

q: What is a standalone construction contract (fristående byggentreprenad)?
a: A standalone construction contract under ABS 18 means the consumer engages a business for construction or installation work on a small house. The contract is governed by both Konsumenttjänstlagen and ABS 18 as a standard contract. The law is mandatory in the consumer's favour.

q: Within what time must the contractor remedy defects?
a: Per ABS 18 clause 22, the contractor must remedy defects within a reasonable time after a complaint. What counts as reasonable depends on the scope of the defect but is usually a few weeks. For extensive defects the time may be longer but must match the nature of the matter.

q: When can the consumer rescind the contract?
a: The consumer can rescind the contract if the defect is of material significance and the contractor does not remedy within a reasonable time, or in the case of material delay per KTjL 21 and 29 §§. For partially performed work, rescission can apply to the remaining part if the purpose of the service is frustrated.

q: What does ARN do in a construction dispute?
a: The National Board for Consumer Disputes (ARN) hears disputes between consumer and business where the value exceeds 2,000 SEK. ARN's decisions are recommendations but are followed by the majority of companies. A complaint must be filed within one year of the first complaint.

q: Can the business refuse to remedy a defect?
a: The business may refuse rectification only if doing so would entail inconvenience or costs for them that are unreasonably large relative to the significance of the defect, per KTjL 20 § 2 st. In such cases the consumer is entitled to a price reduction or damages.

q: How long is the warranty period under AB 04?
a: Under AB 04 kap. 5 § 6, the warranty period is five years for the contractor's workmanship and two years for materials and goods supplied by the contractor. The warranty period starts from approved final inspection.

q: What is the liability period in AB 04?
a: The liability period under AB 04 kap. 5 § 7 is ten years from approved final inspection. During the liability period the contractor is liable for material defects caused by negligence. After the liability period the contractor's contractual liability normally ceases.

q: How long is the warranty period under ABS 18?
a: Under ABS 18 p. 21, the warranty period is two years from approved final inspection. For material defects that appear after the warranty period, the contractor is liable for up to ten years if the defect was caused by negligence. Zaragoza AB always provides warranties under ABS 18.

q: Is the warranty void if I have made changes myself?
a: Yes, if the client or a third party has made interventions that caused the defect, the warranty for that particular defect is void. The contractor is also not liable for defects caused by incorrect care, accident or normal wear and tear under AB 04 kap. 5 § 8.

q: What should I do when the warranty period is about to expire?
a: Request a warranty inspection in writing no later than the end of the warranty period. The inspection is held within a reasonable time thereafter. Defects that the contractor must remedy are recorded during the inspection. Zaragoza AB recommends contacting an inspector well in advance under AB 04 kap. 7 § 3.

q: Can the warranty period be extended?
a: Yes, the parties can agree on a longer warranty than the standard contract's five years. For certain products, such as waterproofing in wet rooms under BKR or GVK, the manufacturer's own extended warranty applies for up to ten or fifteen years, provided the installation was done by an authorized firm.

q: What is the difference between a warranty and the right to complain?
a: A warranty is a voluntary undertaking under which the contractor is liable for defects for a certain time. The right to complain under the Consumer Services Act applies for ten years regardless of warranty, but after the warranty period the burden of proof falls on the consumer to show that the defect is original.

q: Is a building permit required for solar panels?
a: Since 1 August 2018, solar panels that follow the building's form are exempt from permit on one- and two-dwelling houses, unless placed on or within an area of cultural-historic value. Outside detaljplan and within certain areas, a building permit may still be required under PBL 9 kap. 3 c §.

q: What is the green technology deduction?
a: The green technology deduction gives a tax reduction of 20% on material and labour for solar panels (maximum reduction 50,000 SEK per person per year), plus 50% for charging points and battery storage. It is administered via an invoice model where the installer deducts the amount directly.

q: Who is allowed to install solar panels?
a: The electrical installation must be carried out by an electrical installation company registered with Elsäkerhetsverket and with an authorised electrical installer. Panel mounting itself can be done by a builder, but the electrical connection must be performed by an authorised company under Elsäkerhetslagen 2016:732.

q: Is a notification required for an air heat pump?
a: An air-to-air heat pump normally requires neither a building permit nor a notification, but the outdoor unit may require a permit if it substantially alters the façade. An air-to-water heat pump affecting installations often requires a notification under PBF 6 kap. 5 §.

q: How large a roof is needed for a solar panel installation?
a: An average villa needs 25–40 m² of roof for a 5–7 kW installation covering roughly half of its electricity use. South-facing gives the highest output, but east and west also work. Zaragoza AB offers a free roof analysis before solar installation.

q: How large is the ROT deduction on heat pumps?
a: ROT gives a 30% reduction on labour up to 75,000 SEK per person per year (from 2024). Material costs are not included. ROT does not apply simultaneously with the green technology deduction for the same measure.

q: Can I sell surplus electricity from solar panels?
a: Yes, private individuals with microproduction (fuse max 63 A and feed-in max 43.5 kW) get a tax reduction of 60 öre/kWh for surplus electricity fed into the grid, up to 30,000 kWh per year. The electricity supplier additionally pays the market price for the electricity.

q: Is a permit required for geothermal (bergvärme) heating?
a: Yes, installation of bergvärme requires a permit or notification to the municipal environmental office under Miljöbalken 9 kap. and Ordinance 1998:899 on environmentally hazardous activities. Within a water protection area, a permit from the Länsstyrelsen is always required.

q: How deep is a geothermal borehole drilled?
a: A typical drilling depth is 150-250 metres depending on energy demand and geology. A hole normally yields 20-30 kWh per metre per year. For larger properties, several boreholes are required with at least 20 metres between them to avoid thermal interference.

q: What is the difference between bergvärme and jordvärme (ground-source heating)?
a: Bergvärme extracts energy from the bedrock via a vertical borehole. Jordvärme extracts heat via horizontal collector hoses in the ground at 0.9-1.2 metres depth. Bergvärme has more stable temperatures and better efficiency, but requires a drilling permit.

q: Who pays if the neighbour's borehole is damaged?
a: The party performing new drilling is responsible for not damaging existing installations. The SGU-associated industry body recommends at least 20 metres distance from a neighbour's borehole. In the event of damage, damages can be claimed under Miljöbalken 32 kap. or the Tort Liability Act.

q: How long is the processing time for a geothermal permit?
a: The municipality's processing time varies but is normally 4-8 weeks for a notification and up to three months for a permit application. Within water protection areas, where the Länsstyrelsen reviews the case, it may take 3-6 months. Zaragoza AB assists with a complete application.

q: Is Normbrunn certification of the well driller required?
a: Yes, energy wells must be drilled in accordance with SGU's Normbrunn 16 by a certified well driller. Certification is provided through RISE and imposes requirements on equipment, documentation and quality assurance. The building owner should always request a certification certificate.

q: What is the heat-transfer fluid in bergvärme?
a: The heat-transfer fluid is usually bioethanol or ethylene glycol mixed with water, circulating in the collector hose and transporting heat from the bedrock to the heat pump. The fluid must meet the Swedish Environmental Protection Agency's requirements under NFS 2017:5 to reduce environmental risks.

q: Does installing a stove require a building permit?
a: Installation of a fireplace or flue requires a permit-exempt notification to the building committee per PBF 6 kap. 5 §. A start notice is required before installation begins and a final notice once the check is complete. Zaragoza AB handles the notification and inspection plan.

q: What is the legal requirement on chimney sweeping?
a: Under Lag 2003:778 on protection against accidents, sweeping (cleaning) and fire-safety inspection must be carried out at intervals determined by the municipality. The municipality is responsible for sweeping, but the property owner may — under certain conditions — sweep themselves.

q: How often should a fire-safety inspection be done?
a: Per MSBFS 2014:6, fire-safety inspection of fireplaces and flues must be carried out every three or six years depending on the extent of use. The inspection is performed by the municipality's procured chimney-sweep technician and documented in a protocol.

q: What requirements apply to newly installed wood boilers?
a: Per BBR 6:741, newly installed wood boilers must be environmentally approved per EN 303-5 class 5 and fitted with an accumulator tank to reduce emissions. Emission limits for particulates and OGC have been tightened from 2022 under the Ecodesign Directive.

q: Can I burn in an open fireplace in a residential area?
a: Yes, but the municipality may, through local health-protection regulations, restrict wood burning in densely built-up areas. Upon complaint, the environmental committee can intervene per Miljöbalken 9 kap. 3 § if the burning causes a nuisance to human health.

q: What are the requirements on flue distance to combustibles?
a: BBR 5:4241 and the manufacturer's installation instructions specify the clearance between a flue and combustible material. Normally at least 50–100 mm of free air or approved insulation is required. Incorrect distance is the most common cause of chimney fires.

q: Is terraced-house adaptation required for a shared chimney?
a: With a shared flue in a terraced house, each apartment's fireplace must be connected via its own channel to prevent the spread of smoke and fire. A fire-class EI 60 requirement for flues between apartments applies per BBR 5:533 and Boverket's general advice.

q: What working hours apply to construction noise?
a: There is no general Swedish law on working hours for construction, but the municipality's local public order regulations and Naturvårdsverket's guidance NFS 2004:15 on noise from construction sites set reference values. Disturbing noise is typically permitted weekdays 07-19 and Saturdays 08-17.

q: What is the noise limit for construction noise against a dwelling?
a: Naturvårdsverket's reference value is 60 dBA equivalent level during daytime (07-19) at a dwelling's facade. Evenings and nights, 50 and 45 dBA respectively apply. Impulse noise such as sheet piling or percussive drilling must be avoided at night.

q: How are workers protected against noise?
a: Under AFS 2005:16 on noise, the employer must assess risks and take action when noise exceeds 80 dB(A). Hearing protection must be provided and mandatory use applies above 85 dB(A). The maximum permitted exposure is 85 dB(A) as a daily average.

q: What can a neighbour do about nuisance from a construction site?
a: The neighbour can turn to the municipal environmental office, which can intervene under Miljöbalken 9 kap. 3 § if the noise constitutes a nuisance to human health. The environmental committee can order measures or limited working hours if needed.

q: Must the building owner inform neighbours before construction?
a: There is no general obligation, but in the neighbour hearing under PBL 9 kap. 25 § neighbours must be given the opportunity to comment before a permit is granted for a deviation from the detailed plan. For contract works close to a neighbour's property, early dialogue is recommended to avoid disputes. Zaragoza AB always informs neighbours before major works.

q: Can one obtain damages for disturbing noise?
a: Yes, under Miljöbalken 32 kap. 1 §, damages may be awarded for nuisance from an activity exceeding what a neighbour should reasonably tolerate. Damages may cover financial loss, reduced property value and personal discomfort.

q: What requirements apply to sound level in a finished dwelling?
a: BBR 7:2 sets requirements for sound class C per SS 25267 for dwellings. This includes, among other things, a maximum of 30 dBA indoor traffic noise and a maximum of 22 dBA from installations. In new construction, sound measurement must document compliance.

q: Is a building permit required for a deck (altan)?
a: A deck under 1.8 metres above ground normally does not require a permit. A deck higher than that, or one with a roof, may require a permit. Since 2019, a deck is permit-exempt within 3.6 metres from a one- or two-family house if the height is max 1.8 metres, under PBL 9 kap. 4 f §.

q: What is the difference between staket, plank and mur?
a: A staket is a see-through enclosure, usually under 1.1 metres. A plank is a denser and taller enclosure that requires a permit above certain dimensions. A mur is a built wall structure that always requires a permit if higher than 0.5 metres within a detailed-planned area.

q: How tall a staket may I build without a permit?
a: Generally, a staket up to 1.1 metres in height does not require a permit. Above that height it is normally classed as a plank and a permit may be required. The friggebod rule does however allow sheltered patios with a plank up to 1.8 metres within 3.6 m from the dwelling house.

q: What are the attefall rules?
a: The attefall rules grant permit exemption for certain measures on one- and two-family houses: attefallshus 30 sqm, extension 15 sqm, dormer windows (max two) and conversion of an additional dwelling. A notification is required and a startbesked before the work may begin, under PBL 9 kap. 4 a-c §§.

q: May I build a plank on the property line?
a: A plank closer than 4.5 metres from the property line requires the neighbour's consent under the friggebod rule in PBL 9 kap. 4 §. Without consent, a permit is required. Zaragoza AB always recommends written consent to avoid future disputes.

q: Is a permit required for a pool?
a: A pool in itself normally does not require a permit, but a pool enclosure, a deck around the pool, or a safety fence higher than 1.1 metres may require a permit. A safety measure against drowning (at least a 0.9 m fence) is mandatory under Ordningslagen 3 kap. 5 §.

q: What happens if I build a deck without a permit?
a: Unauthorised construction can lead to a byggsanktionsavgift under PBL 11 kap. and PBF 9 kap. The fee is often substantial, and the municipality can order demolition or a retrospective permit application. A permit is, however, not granted if the measure conflicts with the detailed plan.

q: What is an easement (servitut)?
a: An easement is a right for one property (dominant) to use another property (servient) in a certain way, e.g. for road, water or drainage. Easements can be created by agreement or by decision of Lantmäteriet and are regulated in Jordabalken 14 kap.

q: How are property boundaries determined?
a: Property boundaries are established through property formation at Lantmäteriet. The boundary is marked with boundary rods or stones and appears on the property map. In case of doubt, a boundary determination can be requested from Lantmäteriet under Fastighetsbildningslagen 14 kap.

q: Is neighbour consent required when building near the property line?
a: Yes, when building closer than 4.5 metres to the property line, neighbour consent is required to avoid needing a permit under the friggebod rule. For permit-required measures, neighbours must be heard by the building committee if there is a deviation from detaljplan under PBL 9 kap. 25 §.

q: Who maintains a joint facility?
a: A joint facility (e.g. road, well, drainage) is managed by a community association under a facility decision from Lantmäteriet. Maintenance costs are apportioned among the participating properties by share value, regulated by anläggningslagen (1973:1149).

q: Can an easement be terminated?
a: Yes, an easement can be terminated if it is no longer needed, if the parties agree, or through property regulation at Lantmäteriet. Official easements can also be lifted by a court decision if circumstances have changed substantially under Fastighetsbildningslagen 7 kap. 5 §.

q: May the neighbour's tree overhang my property?
a: Under Jordabalken 3 kap. 2 § you may remove branches and roots that intrude onto your property if they cause inconvenience, but only after first requesting the owner to act. The action must not unreasonably harm the tree. Zaragoza AB recommends written dialogue first.

q: What is a right of use (nyttjanderätt)?
a: A right of use is a time-limited right to use another's property, e.g. lease, rental or site leasehold. The right of use is personal, unlike an easement which follows the property, and is regulated in Jordabalken 7–12 kap.

q: What is ID06?
a: ID06 is an industry-wide system for identification, attendance registration and competence management on construction sites. All workers must hold a valid ID06 card under industry practice, and it has been required under Skatteverket's personnel ledger since 2016.

q: What is an authorised electrical installer?
a: An authorised electrical installer is a natural person with technical competence registered with Elsäkerhetsverket. Only electrical installation firms that have an authorised electrical installer for regulatory compliance may carry out electrical installation work under Elsäkerhetslagen 2016:732.

q: What does Säker Vatten authorisation mean?
a: Säker Vatten authorisation means the plumbing firm follows the Säker Vatteninstallation industry rules, has at least one fitter per firm trained and certified, and issues a certificate after installation. Authorisation reduces the risk of water damage and is often required by insurers.

q: What is BF9K?
a: BF9K is a quality, environment and work-environment management system for construction firms developed by Stockholms Byggmästareförening. Certification is done by a third party and shows that the firm has systems equivalent to ISO 9001, ISO 14001 and ISO 45001.

q: Is a trade certificate required for construction workers?
a: A trade certificate is not statutory but is industry practice via Byggnadsindustrins Yrkesnämnd (BYN). The certificate is issued after a completed journeyman test and documented work hours, and is required by many clients. Zaragoza AB always hires trade-trained craftsmen.

q: What is the hot-work (heta arbeten) certificate?
a: A Heta Arbeten certificate is required for anyone performing welding, cutting, soldering or open-flame work at a temporary workplace. The certificate is issued by Brandskyddsföreningen after completed training and is valid for five years. Required by insurers under SBF 506.

q: What is a PBL kontrollansvarig (KA)?
a: A kontrollansvarig under PBL (KA) is a certified person who assists the client in ensuring the control plan is followed. The KA must be certified by an accredited body such as RISE or Kiwa and hold authorisation of normal or complicated complexity under PBL 10 kap. 9 §.
